mappings.rationaleAI: The Enlightenment emphasized reason and empirical inquiry as intellectual origins of revolution (A.5.b); Empiricist, Rationalist, and Kantian interpretations of Newtonian physics developed in the 18th century (B.2.c).

Comprehensive survey from ancient Near Eastern contributions through the Scientific Revolution, 19th-century breakthroughs, and 20th-century developments directly maps to the entire history of science outline (A).

Systematic knowledge-building through testable hypotheses connects to both the history of science (A) and the nature and scope of philosophy of science (B.1); science policy and public communication relate to the social significance of science (B.6).
The rebirth of science in the Renaissance (A.4.a), the influence of advances in printing and technology (A.4.a.ii), and the coexistence of new discoveries and old philosophical views (A.4.a.iii).

The revolution in natural philosophy through Bacon, Descartes, and Galileo (A.4.b.i); establishment of scientific societies and progress in particular fields (A.4.b.ii); the 17th-century debate about methodology (B.2.b).
Islamic Golden Age sciences including astronomy, physics, chemistry, and biology correspond to science in other civilizations, specifically Islamic science (A.3).
Mathematical history connects to Greek scientific attitudes and observation (A.2.a.ii), Islamic mathematical transmission (A.3), and early modern mathematization of nature (A.4).
Key figure in the radical reformulation of natural knowledge through experimental method (A.4.b.i); his work exemplifies the 17th-century debate about scientific methodology (B.2.b).
Development of scientific attitudes including observation, inference, and classification, and the Aristotelian view of nature (A.2.a.ii); classical period philosophy of science (B.2.a).
Newton's Principia represents the culmination of the revolution in natural philosophy (A.4.b.ii); Newtonian physics shaped Empiricist, Rationalist, and Kantian interpretations (B.2.c).
Central figure in the Chemical Revolution and institutional organization of science under the French Revolution, pioneering quantitative chemistry (A.5.c).
Discoveries in electromagnetic induction and electrolysis represent 19th-century progress in physics and chemistry (A.6).

Incompleteness theorems address the formal structures of science and limits on constructing formal analysis of scientific inference (B.3.c); questions about the epistemological status of mathematical propositions (B.5.a).

Healing practices from ancient Greek humoral theory connect to science in ancient Western civilization (A.2); 19th-century germ theory and bacteriology represent 19th-century scientific progress (A.6).
Descartes's deductive approach to scientific methodology and radical reformulation of natural knowledge (A.4.b.i, B.2.b).

Empirical framework of observation, hypothesis, and testing connects to empirical procedures and elements of the scientific enterprise (B.3, B.3.a, B.3.b); falsifiability as a demarcation criterion connects to scientific discovery and validation (B.4.a, B.4.b).
Heliocentric model triggered the Copernican Revolution central to the radical reformulation of natural knowledge (A.4.b.i).

Persian philosopher and physician of the Islamic Golden Age contributing to natural science, medicine, and philosophy corresponds to science in other civilizations, specifically Islamic science (A.3).
Deductive versus inductive reasoning connects to the 17th-century debate about methodology between Bacon and Descartes (B.2.b) and 18th-century Empiricist versus Rationalist interpretations (B.2.c).

Epistemological study of knowledge, justification, empiricism, and rationalism connects to the philosophical status of scientific theory and the epistemological status of scientific propositions (B.5, B.5.a).

Historical development of Western philosophical traditions from ancient through modern, including Platonism, Aristotelianism, empiricism, and rationalism, maps to the historical development of the philosophy of science (B.2).
Survey of rational inquiry across traditions provides context for the historical development of the philosophy of science (B.2).
Greek civilization as the origin of scientific attitudes, disciplined observation, and the Platonic versus Aristotelian view of nature (A.2.a, A.2.a.i, A.2.a.ii).
Increases in trade and exploration placed demands on science and connected to the influence of advances in technology during the Renaissance (A.4.a.ii).
Romanticism as a reaction against Enlightenment rationalism corresponds to the Romantic reaction and science, including the proponents of Naturphilosophie (A.5.d).
Broad survey of human development provides general context for the problems and difficulties of tracing the development of science (A.1).
The early modern period (c. 1500-1800) encompassing the Scientific Revolution and Enlightenment corresponds to European science in the early modern period (A.4).
Turing's formalization of computation in the mid-20th century represents science in the early 20th century -- the social organization and style of science (A.7).
Ibn Khaldun's empirical social analysis and systematic theories of historical causation represent science in other civilizations (A.3).
Influence of advances in printing, mining, and metallurgy on science (A.4.a.ii); science during the Industrial Revolution (A.5.a).
Sociology of knowledge and scientific attitudes connects to the relevance of scientific knowledge to other spheres of human experience and the social significance of science (B.6).
Progress in physics in the 19th century (A.6) and early 20th-century advances in the style and organization of physical science (A.7).

Mathematics as a tool for scientific investigation connects to development of scientific attitudes in Greek civilization (A.2.a.ii) and diverse views of the relative importance of mathematical formulation (B.3.a).

Systematic empirical investigations of anatomy and natural phenomena preceded modern scientific inquiry during the Renaissance (A.4.a), representing the coexistence of new discoveries and old views (A.4.a.iii).
